In 2024, Altrata received 961 data‑subject requests. A large proportion were submitted by third‑party agents. In these cases, proof of the consumer’s identity and a power of attorney signed by the consumer were requested from the agent. When this step isn’t completed, we can only partially proceed and cannot fulfill the request in full. Separately, the denials reported reflect requests where we could not locate a matching record in our systems.

We received 9 requests to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information. Because these requests included reference to sensitive personal information we have included them in this number. However, please note, we do not process such categories of personal information. 

Looking ahead, we expect the state’s DROP system to improve full‑fulfillment rates because requests made through DROP include consumer identity verification at the outset. Our goal is always to fulfill valid requests in full, and we are updating our internal playbooks to align with DROP so we can process verified requests more quickly and consistently.

column79:
Candl is a birthday reminder app to help individuals keep track of their contacts’ birthdays. When a user signs up for Candl, they must provide their phone number and birth day and month. They can also grant Candl access to contacts in their address book; if they do, Candl will store the phone number(s) and birth day and month, if any, associated with each of those contacts. When a user gives the app access to contact information in their address book, if that user has the Birthday Network feature enabled, Candl stores and periodically syncs from that address book each contact’s day and month (but not year) of birth in its Birthday Network in association with the contact’s phone number; the app does the same for the user’s own birth day and month. If another Candl user has the Birthday Network feature enabled and gives Candl access to the same contact without an associated birthday, Candl will share the prevailing birthday from the Birthday Network with that other Candl user.

Because Candl is designed to collect only limited personal data, Baron App has little insight into the contacts included in the Birthday Network or the app users accessing that data, and does not independently verify the identity of app users beyond validating that they can receive text messages at the phone number they use to sign up for the app. Users of the app must represent and warrant that they will not use Candl, and will not assist or encourage any third party to use Candl, to access or attempt to access any information (including birthdays and phone numbers) associated with any individual they do not personally know, and that they will not use Candl, and will not assist or encourage any third party to use Candl, to extract, export, withdraw, or otherwise remove any information from the Candl app or attempt to do any of the foregoing.

BDO’s Global Corporate Intelligence services were previously registered as part of BDO USA, P.C., which is no longer a data broker under California law. BDO GCI, LLC was formed in January 2025, and did not receive any data subject requests in 2024. For the period beginning on January 1, 2024, and ending on December 31, 2024, BDO has compiled the following statistics regarding individual requests to know, access, delete, and opt out of sale, sharing and/or targeted advertising: BDO received 48,984 total requests, complied with 42,936 of these requests in whole or in part, denied 6,048 requests and responded to these requests with a median response timeline of 0 days and a mean of 1 day. Of these requests, 2 deletion requests involved data that BDO USA did not delete pursuant to exceptions under Sections 1798.145 or 1798.146 of the California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018. For this request, BDO USA relied on the exceptions covering our ability to “comply with federal, state, or local laws,” “comply with a civil, criminal, or regulatory inquiry, investigation, subpoena, or summons by federal, state, or local authorities,” and “exercise or defend legal claims.” Please note that the number of “Denied” requests includes abandoned requests by the consumer and failed identity verification attempts. In particular, BDO has received large numbers of deletion requests from authorized agents that have been nonresponsive to our attempts to process these requests. Additionally, please note that these metrics include requests we received at the end of 2024 and responded to in 2025.

Under HIPAA, covered entities include health plans, health care clearinghouses, and health care providers. Business associates are generally defined as entities that create, receive, maintain, or transmit individually identifiable health information (“PHI”) on behalf of covered entities, or that provide certain services involving the disclosure of PHI to covered entities. 
Some Blackbaud customers are covered entities or business associates that input PHI to Blackbaud products and solutions through their use of Blackbaud products and services. In such circumstances, Blackbaud is functioning in the capacity of a HIPAA-defined business associate.  

Blackbaud does not sell or share PHI.

Blueprint Audiences processes limited pseudonymous identifiers for the purpose of creating and validating audience segments for advertising activation.

Blueprint maintains a hashed identifier received from a third-party data provider. Blueprint does not collect or store direct personal identifiers such as names, email addresses, phone numbers, postal addresses, Social Security numbers, or precise geolocation data. Blueprint does not possess the additional information required to directly identify an individual or to reverse the hashed identifier.

The identifiers processed by Blueprint are used to support aggregate analysis and validation of defined populations. Blueprint does not collect information directly from consumers and does not maintain a direct relationship with consumers.

Blueprint does not attempt to reidentify individuals, does not request additional information for identification purposes, and does not retain personal information solely for the purpose of responding to consumer rights requests. Blueprint maintains policies and procedures designed to assess and respond to consumer requests in accordance with applicable law.

Consumer Canvas provides businesses with access to data that helps them better understand the U.S. consumer and improve their abilities to understand consumers' interests and preferences, primarily so they send ads that are more likely to be of interest to their consumers. Consumer Canvas does not collect its data directly from consumers; instead, it obtains information collected and provided by its data provider partners.   
When Consumer Canvas sells, shares, or discloses personal information, it takes contractual, technical, and organizational measures that are designed to limit the recipients’ use to specific purposes that are consistent with its privacy notice and in accordance with applicable law.
Consumer Canvas empowers and supports the exercise of consumer privacy rights. At any time, consumers can submit privacy requests by visiting: https://submit-irm.trustarc.com/services/validation/f7c5ccce-aaa8-442a-8c67-0ddc1558a5d9 (also available by selecting "Your Privacy Choices" in the footer of the website: www.consumercanvas.net).

Virtually all denials of deletion and opt-out requests were due to third-parties that submitted opt-out/deletion requests on behalf of consumers but did not provide information of the consumer (such as an email address) needed to process the request.

The number of opt-outs fulfilled may in certain reporting periods exceed the total volume of opt-out requests received because Evorra adds users who have requested data to be deleted to our opt-out list, even if the user did not specifically ask to be added to Evorra’s opt-out list.

We received 112 California privacy requests, and they were largely from third-party companies that purport to assist with these requests. We understand "complied in part" to mean that, in connection with a request to delete or opt out, we reached out to request basic information (like email and phone number, which is the data we would potentially have) and implement the request, as needed. We did not receive any responses. As for the "denied" requests, we were able to investigate those requests and confirm that we lacked any data about the consumer, meaning there was nothing to delete. It is unclear whether that is properly categorized as a "denied" request, so we provide this explanation. Though unrelated, we also implemented deletion requests for consumers in states outside California.

Please note there is a major discrepancy between the numbers of requests Famous Birthdays LLC ("Famous Birthdays") receives. This is because Famous Birthdays uses consumer personal information in two distinct ways:

1. Running an advertising-supported public website, which shares consumer information with advertising agencies; and
2. Maintaining and licensing access to a database of information concerning celebrities, the overwhelming majority of which is "publicly available" information under the CCPA.

Use #1 attracts large numbers of opts outs from selling/sharing, which are received through Famous Birthdays' consent management platform (including via global privacy control) and processed instantaneously. 

Use #2 attracts very modest numbers of requests to delete from the profiled celebrities, which are processed manually. Please note that it is only use #2 that qualifies Famous Birthdays as a data broker. 

Information is being provided about both uses because we understand that to be required, but we would note that conflating the two kinds of data uses is more likely to confuse consumers than it is to inform them.

Separately, we'd ask that the CPPA uses its regulatory authority to reduce the burdens this process places on small businesses like Famous Birthdays that only barely qualify as Data Brokers under the law and appear to be on the margins of what the State is seeking to regulate with this process. The reporting could be better directed to only those uses implicated by a business' data brokerage operations, and could also levy fees that are better tailored to business' ability to pay. On the latter score, note that the current fees are extremely onerous on small business.

column79: All the data mentioned above is relevant to the year 2024.   Snovio receives and processes Data subject access requests (DSAR) which are formal inquiries made by individuals to a company. It involves the data subject seeking information regarding collecting, storing, and using their personal data. Any individual who qualifies as a data subject can submit such a request. We cannot measure the volume of personal data processed in such requests; we can only ensure its deletion upon request. Users can request that the company delete all the data associated with them, a right commonly referred to as the right to erasure or the right to be forgotten. Snovio only collects specific categories of data that are essential to providing our services. This includes: Email Addresses: For identifying individuals within our platform. Names: To provide personalized results and accurate matching. Company Information: Such as company name, position, and industry for professional networking purposes. Publicly Available Information: From open sources, such as websites or social profiles, used in compliance with applicable privacy laws. Snovio does not collect sensitive personal data unless explicitly provided by the user for service-related purposes. How Can Users Request Data Deletion? To request the deletion of their data, users can either: Contact us directly by sending an email to help@snov.io. Fill out the online form https://app.snov.io/clear-email opt-out narrative: If you do not want your personal data to be included as part of our service, you may opt-out by contacting us at help@snov.io or opt-out from all mailing lists on the Platform through this form. You can read more in the ‘HOW TO DELETE MY DATA’ section of this Policy. , which provides an automated way to manage their request. What Can Users Request? A summary of the information the company stores about them. Details on whether anyone has had access to their data. Deletion of all personal data from the system. We are committed to processing these requests promptly and in full compliance with applicable data privacy regulations.

In 2024, we received 58,307 deletion requests. Of these, 16,514 resulted in actual deletion of records, and 39,480 requests were fulfilled by confirming that no personal information existed for the consumer.  Both scenarios are reported as "complied in full", as the request was fully honored.  We reported 2,313 requests "denied" because the information provided was insufficient to verify the consumer's identity.  No requests were partially fulfilled.  
 
In 2024, we received 320 opt-out requests. Of these, 46 resulted in opt-outs, and 167 requests were fulfilled by confirming no personal information existed for the consumer.  Both scenarios are reported as "complied in full", as the request was fully honored.  We reported 107 "denied" because the information provided was insufficient to verify the consumer's identity. column79: Unacast places an emphasis on being open and transparent about our data processing activities. Our team is regularly available to address and discuss any privacy concerns regarding our services. A copy of our regularly-updated privacy policy is also available on our website.
